steps.title: Spot the comma splice
steps.content:
The sentence contains two independent clauses separated only by a comma:

Clause 1: “This conceptualization of harmonic opposites did not originate with Collier in 1985”
Clause 2: “the idea was first articulated in Levy's book”

A comma alone cannot join two independent clauses—this is a comma splice error.

steps.title: Apply the semicolon rule
steps.content:
Two independent clauses require a semicolon (not a comma) to separate them properly.

Choice B places a semicolon after “though,” — where “though” serves as a parenthetical word expressing concession (like “admittedly”) right after “Collier.” The semicolon then divides the two clauses: ...did not originate with Collier, though; in 1985, the idea was first articulated... The other choices either leave the comma splice unsolved (A and C both retain “...in 1985, the idea” as a continuation after a comma) or place punctuation incorrectly (D uses the conjunctive adverb pattern poorly here).

This matches choice B — B is correct.
